leadTime.what: The total time from when a feature is requested (Jira ticket created) to when it's live in production. It covers the full journey: waiting, coding, reviewing, testing, and deploying.
leadTime.why: Lead time shows how fast your team delivers value to users. Long lead times often mean too much waiting — not too much coding.
leadTime.good: Less than 2 days — your pipeline is fast and friction-free.
leadTime.okay: 2 to 5 days — decent, but look for bottlenecks in review or deployment.
leadTime.bad: More than 5 days — something is slowing you down. Check queue times and handoffs.

cycleTime.what: The time from when a developer starts working on a task (first commit or branch created) to when it's merged and deployed. Unlike lead time, this doesn't include the waiting period before work begins.
cycleTime.why: Cycle time measures your actual engineering speed. If cycle time is low but lead time is high, the problem isn't coding — it's the queue before coding starts.
cycleTime.good: Less than 1 day — code is moving fast from dev to production.
cycleTime.okay: 1 to 3 days — normal for most teams, but PRs might be sitting in review too long.
cycleTime.bad: More than 3 days — PRs are likely too large, reviews are slow, or CI is failing frequently.
cycleTime.source: GitHub (first commit → PR merge) + CI/CD (merge → deploy)

bugRate.what: The percentage of deployments or changes that introduced a bug reported within 7 days. Calculated as (bugs found / total changes deployed) × 100.
bugRate.why: A high bug rate means code is shipping without enough testing or review. It costs more to fix bugs in production than to catch them before deployment.
bugRate.good: Less than 2% — your quality gates are working well.
bugRate.okay: 2% to 5% — typical, but worth investigating which types of changes cause bugs.
bugRate.bad: More than 5% — serious quality issue. Check test coverage, review depth, and deployment practices.
bugRate.source: Bug tracker (Jira bugs linked to releases) + CI/CD (deployment count)

deploymentFrequency.what: How many times your code is deployed to production in a week. This is one of the four DORA metrics used by Google to measure engineering team performance.
deploymentFrequency.why: Frequent deployments mean smaller changes, lower risk per release, and faster feedback. Teams that deploy often tend to have fewer incidents.
deploymentFrequency.good: More than 15 per week — you're shipping fast with small, safe changes.
deploymentFrequency.okay: 5 to 15 per week — solid, but there may be room to break work into smaller deployable units.
deploymentFrequency.bad: Less than 5 per week — large batch releases increase risk. Consider CI/CD improvements.
deploymentFrequency.source: CI/CD pipeline (successful production deployments per week)

prThroughput.what: The total number of pull requests opened and merged in a given period. This measures how actively code is being reviewed and integrated.
prThroughput.why: PR throughput reflects collaboration health. Low throughput might mean PRs are too big (hard to review), reviewers are overloaded, or developers are blocked.
prThroughput.good: More than 40 PRs per sprint — healthy flow of small, reviewable changes.
prThroughput.okay: 20 to 40 PRs per sprint — decent, but check if PRs are sitting idle in review.
prThroughput.bad: Less than 20 PRs per sprint — possible bottleneck. Are PRs too large? Are reviewers available?
prThroughput.source: GitHub (pull request open/merge events per sprint)
